REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is mostly reproducible: datasets (CoNLL 2008/2009, Gigaword) are public, hyperparameters and model configurations are reported, and code is available. However the summary indicates some preprocessing/transition-encoding edge cases (the 1.5% of sentences with no valid transition sequence) and several hyperparameters were set "intuitively" which suggests not all tuning details are fully specified. Given the available code and reported metrics, a competent implementer should replicate results, but minor implementation and preprocessing specifics may require clarification or inspection of the released code. | B(4): Given the summary, this paper is mostly reproducible: datasets are standard (CoNLL 2008/2009, Gigaword), hyperparameters are reported, and code is available which strongly aids replication. The model architecture (four stack LSTMs, transition system, greedy linear-time inference) and evaluation metric (F1) are clearly stated. Minor gaps remain—e.g., a few implementation-level preprocessing or transition-encoding corner cases (the 1.5% mis-encoding) and some hyperparameter choices described as "intuitive" rather than fully justified—so exact parity may require contacting authors or inspecting the released code.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is mostly reproducible: datasets (CoNLL 2008/2009, Gigaword) are public, hyperparameters and model configurations are reported, and code is available. However the summary indicates some preprocessing/transition-encoding edge cases (the 1.5% of sentences with no valid transition sequence) and several hyperparameters were set "intuitively" which suggests not all tuning details are fully specified. Given the available code and reported metrics, a competent implementer should replicate results, but minor implementation and preprocessing specifics may require clarification or inspection of the released code.

reviewer_b.justification: Given the summary, this paper is mostly reproducible: datasets are standard (CoNLL 2008/2009, Gigaword), hyperparameters are reported, and code is available which strongly aids replication. The model architecture (four stack LSTMs, transition system, greedy linear-time inference) and evaluation metric (F1) are clearly stated. Minor gaps remain—e.g., a few implementation-level preprocessing or transition-encoding corner cases (the 1.5% mis-encoding) and some hyperparameter choices described as "intuitive" rather than fully justified—so exact parity may require contacting authors or inspecting the released code.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad set of relevant and recent baselines (Henderson et al. 2013, Lluís et al. 2013, Lewis et al. 2015, and other strong systems) on the same CoNLL 2008/2009 data and F1 metrics, which is appropriate. However, while related work coverage is adequate, the empirical comparisons appear to omit statistical significance testing and finer-grained analyses that would clarify when and why the proposed greedy joint parser outperforms (or fails relative to) competitors—so I judge the comparisons good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper evaluates on standard CoNLL 2008/2009 benchmarks using F1 and compares to the major prior joint syntax–semantics systems (Henderson et al. 2013, Lluís et al. 2013, Lewis et al. 2015) as well as strong neural and classic baselines, and it reports hyperparameters and releases code — strong signals for fair, reproducible comparison. From a practical standpoint the evaluation appears fair (same data and metrics) and emphasizes runtime/greedy linear-time advantage. I dock one point because the summary doesn’t explicitly state whether and where prior methods outperform this approach or detailed error analyses contrasting failures across models, so comparisons, while broad and reproducible, may omit some nuanced head-to-head failure-mode analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of relevant and recent baselines (Henderson et al. 2013, Lluís et al. 2013, Lewis et al. 2015, and other strong systems) on the same CoNLL 2008/2009 data and F1 metrics, which is appropriate. However, while related work coverage is adequate, the empirical comparisons appear to omit statistical significance testing and finer-grained analyses that would clarify when and why the proposed greedy joint parser outperforms (or fails relative to) competitors—so I judge the comparisons good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on standard CoNLL 2008/2009 benchmarks using F1 and compares to the major prior joint syntax–semantics systems (Henderson et al. 2013, Lluís et al. 2013, Lewis et al. 2015) as well as strong neural and classic baselines, and it reports hyperparameters and releases code — strong signals for fair, reproducible comparison. From a practical standpoint the evaluation appears fair (same data and metrics) and emphasizes runtime/greedy linear-time advantage. I dock one point because the summary doesn’t explicitly state whether and where prior methods outperform this approach or detailed error analyses contrasting failures across models, so comparisons, while broad and reproducible, may omit some nuanced head-to-head failure-mode analysis.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions—the transition-based joint parser and the stack LSTM state encoding—are stated early and developed through method and experiments. Notation and examples appear consistent and figures/tables informative; hyperparameters and code availability are clearly reported and related work coverage is adequate. From my perspective, a minor presentation gap is the lack of a formal argument or explicit complexity sketch accompanying the linear-time claim and the greedy inference design (important for readers focused on guarantees), but writing quality is otherwise good and the empirical setup is clearly communicated. | B(4): The paper is well organized and presents the approach in a logical sequence (intro → method → experiments → conclusion), with the joint transition-based parser and its greedy linear-time inference stated clearly early on. Notation and model components (four stack LSTMs for S, M, B, and action history) are defined and consistent, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate the method and results on CoNLL 2008/2009. Writing is generally clear and grammatically sound, with useful reproducibility signals (code and hyperparameters reported); minor issues remain (e.g., a small fraction of transition-sequence encoding errors and several hyperparameters set intuitively) but these do not substantially hinder comprehension.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions—the transition-based joint parser and the stack LSTM state encoding—are stated early and developed through method and experiments. Notation and examples appear consistent and figures/tables informative; hyperparameters and code availability are clearly reported and related work coverage is adequate. From my perspective, a minor presentation gap is the lack of a formal argument or explicit complexity sketch accompanying the linear-time claim and the greedy inference design (important for readers focused on guarantees), but writing quality is otherwise good and the empirical setup is clearly communicated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and presents the approach in a logical sequence (intro → method → experiments → conclusion), with the joint transition-based parser and its greedy linear-time inference stated clearly early on. Notation and model components (four stack LSTMs for S, M, B, and action history) are defined and consistent, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate the method and results on CoNLL 2008/2009. Writing is generally clear and grammatically sound, with useful reproducibility signals (code and hyperparameters reported); minor issues remain (e.g., a small fraction of transition-sequence encoding errors and several hyperparameters set intuitively) but these do not substantially hinder comprehension.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and delivers strong empirical gains with a fast, reproducible linear-time greedy system using stack LSTMs. The work is likely to influence practitioners due to its efficiency and released code, but it is not a theoretical breakthrough and would be strengthened by ablation studies, significance testing, or global-training/beam-search analyses. Given the clear practical impact despite methodological limitations, I assign a score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing and presents a practically attractive, linear-time transition system that uses four stack LSTMs to represent global parser state and reportedly outperforms prior joint models on CoNLL 2008/2009. From a theory-minded perspective the contribution is useful — it shows that a compact, greedy, neural-state encoding can yield strong empirical results — but its longer-term influence is limited because the method relies on greedy inference without accompanying global-training, beam-search, or provable guarantees, and the summary lacks mention of ablation studies or statistical significance testing to isolate the role of the four stacks and the joint transition design. These methodological gaps mean the work is a solid, practically relevant advance but not yet transformative for how we reason about constrained joint structured prediction."} PMID:NULL}{

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—efficient joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing—and demonstrates empirically superior performance on standard CoNLL 2008/2009 benchmarks using a linear-time greedy transition system. The use of stack LSTMs to represent full parser state and the release of code and hyperparameters make the approach reproducible and attractive for practitioners who value speed and simplicity. While not a theoretical breakthrough, its strong empirical gains and practical efficiency are likely to change how systems are built and to be widely adopted or extended in follow-up work.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper clearly states its claims and provides empirical evidence of improved F1 with a solid experimental setup and released code, but they also note limitations such as lack of formal proofs, limited analysis of greedy decoding, and missing ablation/statistical significance testing. These shortcomings are important but do not negate the empirical contribution; therefore a score slightly above middle (4) reflects the paper's strong empirical results tempered by methodological gaps that should be addressed in revision.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and presents experiments showing superior F1 on CoNLL 2008/2009 using a stack-LSTM based greedy joint transition parser, but several technical weaknesses remain. There is no formal proof of the claimed linear-time inference or of correctness for jointly constrained outputs (only an empirical note that 1.5% of sentences are mis-encoded), and the method relies on greedy decoding without accompanying global training/beam-search guarantees. The summary also lacks mention of ablation studies isolating the four stack-LSTM components and no statistical significance testing is reported, so empirical gains are insufficiently analyzed to fully support the conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims—a stack-LSTM based, transition-style joint syntax+SRL parser with linear-time greedy inference—are clearly stated and empirically supported by superior F1 on CoNLL-2008/2009 and comparisons to strong baselines (Henderson et al., Lluís et al., Lewis et al.). Mathematical apparatus is lightweight and consistent with prior transition-based work; no formal proofs are required for the empirical claim. Experimental methodology is generally sound: appropriate datasets, strong baselines, released code and reported hyperparameters increase reproducibility. Minor technical caveats remain (1.5% of sentences cannot be encoded by the transition sequence; heuristic handling of unseen predicate senses), but these do not undermine the main empirical conclusions.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is squarely within the conference scope, evaluates on standard CoNLL benchmarks, compares to strong baselines, and provides code and reproducible details. While Reviewer A reasonably flags methodological limitations (greedy transition-based inference without global training, beam search, or targeted ablations), these concerns do not outweigh the paper’s clear practical contributions, strong empirical results, and emphasis on efficiency and reproducibility, so I rate it a strong fit (5).

reviewer_a.justification: This work is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: joint syntactic+semantic dependency parsing with neural stack-LSTM state encodings, evaluated on CoNLL 2008/2009 against strong baselines and with code released. However, from my theory-minded perspective the reliance on greedy, transition-based inference without accompanying global-training, beam search, or formal guarantees (and no reported ablation isolating the four stack LSTMs and joint design) raises methodological concerns that keep it from being a perfect fit. Overall it addresses the right audience and uses expected datasets and metrics, but I would expect stronger analysis or guarantees for a top-tier acceptance.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it tackles joint syntactic+semantic parsing with a practical, efficient transition-based model that uses stack LSTMs and greedy linear-time inference, and reports superior performance on standard CoNLL 2008/2009 benchmarks. The evaluation uses community-standard metrics (F1), compares to appropriate strong baselines, provides code and hyperparameters, and emphasizes wall-clock/algorithmic efficiency and reproducibility—qualities I value as a practitioner seeking impactful, deployable advances.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the work integrates known components rather than proposing a fundamentally new theory, but they also concur the specific combination (synchronized transition system with four stack LSTMs and greedy linear-time inference) is a non-trivial, practically useful advancement. Given the clear differentiation from prior work, empirical gains, and reproducibility, the contribution merits a positive originality score while acknowledging it is primarily an engineering and methodological extension rather than a radical innovation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines existing components (a Henderson-style joint transition system and stack LSTMs to encode parser state) into a single greedy, linear-time joint syntactic+semantic parser. That integration yields an efficient and empirically strong system, but it is mainly an engineering of known ideas (transition-based parsing, stack LSTMs, linear-time inference) rather than a fundamentally new formulation or algorithmic guarantee. The authors do clearly differentiate from prior graph-based and CCG joint approaches and from Henderson et al., but the technique is a non-trivial yet incremental extension that an expert would find plausible rather than surprising.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies stack LSTMs in a synchronized transition-based framework to jointly produce syntactic trees and SRL graphs with a linear-time greedy parser — a clear methodological novelty over prior joint models (e.g., Henderson et al., Lluís et al.). The joint formulation itself is well-studied, but the specific combination (four stack LSTMs encoding S, M, B, and action history) and the practical claim of superior CoNLL 2008/2009 performance with greedy inference is a notable, non-trivial extension. The authors clearly differentiate their approach from earlier transition- and graph-based joint systems and provide reproducibility signals (code, hyperparameters), so the contribution would be informative to practitioners despite not reinventing the problem formulation.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the experimental evaluation is solid, reproducible, and discusses limitations, but they also concur that important analyses are missing (ablation/sensitivity studies, significance testing, and runtime/efficiency measurements). Because these omissions limit the paper’s theoretical and practical claims despite strong empirical breadth, I give a middle score reflecting solid work but insufficient analysis to merit a higher rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The empirical evaluation is adequate in scope (CoNLL-2008/2009, multiple strong baselines, pretrained embeddings, code and hyperparameters released), but crucial analyses are missing from a theory-oriented perspective. The paper reports a greedy, linear-time parser yet provides no formal argument or empirical ablation isolating the contribution of each stack LSTM (S, M, B, action history) or the joint transition design, and there is no mention of statistical significance testing for the reported gains. Related work and limitations are discussed and some failure modes are acknowledged (predicate-sense defaulting, 1.5% irreconcilable sequences), and reproducibility is supported by released code and hyperparameters. Overall: solid experimental breadth but lacking the ablations, significance tests, and formal/empirical guarantees I would require.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the experimental evaluation is solid: the model is evaluated on CoNLL-2008/2009 with pretrained embeddings, compared against a broad set of strong baselines, and code plus hyperparameters are released which supports reproducibility. The paper reports and quantifies specific limitations (the 1.5% encoding failures and the unseen-predicate fallback) and covers related work adequately. However, the summary does not mention ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., contribution of stack LSTMs, action-history features, or greedy vs. beam decoding) nor wall‑clock/throughput measurements to substantiate practical efficiency claims, so some important analyses are missing.

main_claims: A transition-based, incremental greedy parser that jointly produces syntactic dependency trees and semantic role (SRL) graphs using a synchronized transition system.
The model learns a finite-length vector representation of the entire parser state via stack LSTMs (for S, M, B, and action history) to select transitions.
The greedy inference algorithm (including feature extraction) runs in linear time and achieves superior performance to prior joint syntax–semantics models on the CoNLL 2008 and 2009 English tasks.
_summary.methodology: A transition-based joint parser that encodes the parser state with four stack LSTMs (syntactic stack, semantic stack, buffer, action history) and uses a classifier (linear scoring with learned parameters) over allowed transitions to greedily construct both syntactic and semantic dependency structures.
limitations: For 1.5% of English sentences in the CoNLL 2009 dataset, the transition sequence incorrectly encodes the gold-standard joint parse.
Unseen predicate senses at test time are handled by defaulting to the “01” sense (which is correct 91.22% of predicates by type in the CoNLL 2009 English training data).
Many hyperparameters were set intuitively and authors note careful tuning could improve results.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing

reviewer_a.name: Theory-minded Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in computational linguistics and a track record of theoretical work on parsing algorithms and formal guarantees.
traits: penalizes papers that present greedy inference without accompanying global-training, beam-search, or provable guarantees
requires ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each model component (here: each of the four stack LSTMs and the joint transition design) and will downgrade if missing
penalizes lack of statistical significance testing for empirical gains over baselines
expects clear complexity and correctness proofs or at least formal argumentation when claiming linear-time inference for jointly constrained outputs
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on whether the method advances fundamental understanding (algorithms/guarantees) and demand rigorous ablations and analysis, often ruling against empirically-driven incremental engineering absent such evidence.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Empiricalist
reviewer_b.background: Experienced applied NLP researcher/engineer who has led production deployments of parsers and co-authored multiple empirical papers improving parsing/semantic role labeling benchmarks.
traits: rewards papers that report clear empirical improvements on standard benchmarks (e.g., CoNLL 2008/2009) and provide wall-clock or algorithmic efficiency claims (e.g., linear-time greedy inference)
favors simple, efficient architectures that enable practical gains (will upscore models that are greedy/linear-time if accuracy is strong)
gives positive weight to reproducibility signals (release of code, hyperparameters, or detailed implementation notes) and to comparisons against strong neural baselines
is tolerant of lack of formal guarantees if the empirical evaluation is thorough and surpasses prior joint syntax–semantics systems
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize empirical impact, usability, and strong benchmark performance; practical simplicity and speed that deliver measurable gains are persuasive to me even without deep theoretical guarantees.
